summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Nero Recode 2 as the superior DVD ripping solution after experiencing unreliable results with alternatives like XMPEG and Dr. DivX. The software offers dramatically faster encoding speeds (90fps vs 50fps), better stability with no crashes, and superior video quality according to expert reviews. However, Nero's proprietary 'Nero Digital' format creates compatibility headaches, requiring either the bundled Nero Showtime player or a third-party decoder like 3ivx to play the resulting files. Despite this annoyance, Atwood strongly recommends the Nero Ultra suite as best-of-breed software for DVD encoding tasks.
key_points: Nero Recode 2 provides dramatically faster encoding (90fps vs 50fps) and superior stability compared to free alternatives like XMPEG or Dr. DivX
MPEG4 encoding can compress 9GB of DVD data to 700MB while maintaining near-DVD quality, thanks to being a more modern codec than MPEG2
Nero Digital format uses advanced MPEG4 with AAC audio but lacks broad decoder support, forcing users to install Nero Showtime or pay for third-party decoders
The K-Lite Codec Pack offers a free workaround for Nero Digital playback in Windows Media Player
Video encoding remains one of the few tasks where raw CPU power still matters significantly
money_quotes: I just ripped about 10 different DVDs with Nero Recode and it's been a painless point and click operation every single time – without a single crash!
I know it sounds implausible, but I can tell you from personal experience that it works, because MPEG4 is a more modern and efficient codec than MPEG2.
You're forced to install the annoying Nero Showtime application on any PC you want to watch your *.mp4 files from. Not acceptable.
Paying $7 for a decoder irks me.
Video encoding is one of the last great frontiers where PCs can never be fast enough.
We should be able to download a small set of decoders and watch our *.mp4 files in any application we want.
key_insight: Superior software tools should prioritize reliability and ease of use, but proprietary formats that lock users into specific players undermine otherwise excellent products.
